E100.name: Curcumin
E100.summary: Yellow colour extracted from turmeric. Widely regarded as safe at normal food-use levels.

E101.name: Riboflavin (Vitamin B2)
E101.summary: Yellow colour, also a vitamin. No known safety concerns.

E102.name: Tartrazine
E102.summary: Synthetic azo dye. EU requires a warning label for hyperactivity in children; can trigger reactions in people with aspirin sensitivity or asthma.

conditions.adhd: May be linked to increased hyperactivity in sensitive children (Southampton study); EU mandates a warning label.
conditions.allergies: Known trigger for intolerance reactions, particularly in people with asthma or aspirin sensitivity.

E104.name: Quinoline Yellow
E104.summary: Synthetic dye carrying the same EU hyperactivity warning as E102.

conditions.adhd: Part of the 'Southampton Six' linked to hyperactivity; EU warning label required.

E110.name: Sunset Yellow FCF
E110.summary: Synthetic azo dye; one of the 'Southampton Six' colours requiring an EU hyperactivity warning.

conditions.adhd: Linked to hyperactivity in children; EU warning label required.
conditions.allergies: Can trigger reactions in people sensitive to azo dyes or aspirin.

E120.name: Cochineal / Carmine
E120.summary: Red colour extracted from cochineal insects. Not suitable for vegans/vegetarians; rare but severe allergic reactions reported.

conditions.allergies: Documented cases of anaphylaxis; not vegan/vegetarian/halal-kosher in some interpretations.

E122.name: Carmoisine
E122.summary: Synthetic azo dye, part of the 'Southampton Six'.

conditions.adhd: Linked to hyperactivity; EU warning label required.

E123.name: Amaranth
E123.summary: Banned in the US since 1976 over cancer concerns in animal studies; still permitted in small amounts in the EU.

conditions.pregnancy: Advised to avoid due to historical carcinogenicity concerns.

E124.name: Ponceau 4R
E124.summary: Synthetic azo dye, part of the 'Southampton Six'.

conditions.adhd: Linked to hyperactivity; EU warning label required.
conditions.allergies: Can trigger reactions in aspirin-sensitive individuals.

E129.name: Allura Red AC
E129.summary: Synthetic azo dye, part of the 'Southampton Six'; banned in some countries.

conditions.adhd: Linked to hyperactivity; EU warning label required.

E131.name: Patent Blue V
E131.summary: Synthetic dye; can cause allergic reactions and, rarely, anaphylaxis at high doses (e.g. in medical dye use).

E133.name: Brilliant Blue FCF
E133.summary: Synthetic blue dye, generally considered low-risk; banned in a few countries as a precaution.

E140.name: Chlorophylls
E140.summary: Green colour from plants. No known safety concerns.

E150a.name: Plain Caramel
E150a.summary: Simple caramel colour. Generally recognised as safe.

E150c.name: Ammonia Caramel
E150c.summary: Caramel colour made with ammonia compounds; used widely in colas and sauces, considered safe at regulated levels.

E150d.name: Sulphite Ammonia Caramel
E150d.summary: Contains 4-MEI, a compound under review for possible carcinogenicity at very high exposure; California requires a warning above certain levels.

E160a.name: Beta-carotene
E160a.summary: Orange colour, precursor to vitamin A. No known safety concerns.

E160b.name: Annatto
E160b.summary: Natural orange-red colour; rare allergic reactions reported (urticaria, anaphylaxis).

E163.name: Anthocyanins
E163.summary: Natural purple/red colour from berries and grape skins. No known safety concerns.

E171.name: Titanium Dioxide
E171.summary: White colourant; banned as a food additive in the EU since 2022 after EFSA could not rule out genotoxicity concerns. Still permitted in some other markets.

E200.name: Sorbic Acid
E200.summary: Common mould/yeast inhibitor. Generally well tolerated.

E202.name: Potassium Sorbate
E202.summary: Widely used preservative, low toxicity, occasional mild skin/allergy sensitivity.

E211.name: Sodium Benzoate
E211.summary: Common preservative; can form benzene (a carcinogen) when combined with vitamin C under heat/light. Also part of the additive-hyperactivity research alongside colours.

conditions.adhd: Studied alongside artificial colours for a possible link to hyperactivity when combined with E-number dyes.

E220.name: Sulphur Dioxide
E220.summary: Common in dried fruit and wine. A well-known trigger for asthma and sulphite-sensitivity reactions; must be declared as an allergen in the EU above 10mg/kg.

conditions.allergies: Recognised EU allergen; can trigger asthma attacks and anaphylactoid reactions in sensitive individuals.
conditions.hypertension: Not directly linked, but often found in processed/cured foods that are high in sodium.

E223.name: Sodium Metabisulphite
E223.summary: Sulphite preservative; same asthma/allergy concerns as E220.

conditions.allergies: Recognised EU allergen; sulphite sensitivity can trigger asthma.

E249.name: Potassium Nitrite
E249.summary: Used in cured meats; can form nitrosamines (probable carcinogens) during cooking at high heat. WHO classifies processed meat with nitrites as Group 1 carcinogenic.

conditions.hypertension: Found in cured/processed meats which are typically also high in sodium.

E250.name: Sodium Nitrite
E250.summary: Used in cured/processed meats (bacon, ham, salami) to prevent botulism; associated with nitrosamine formation and linked by WHO to colorectal cancer risk.

conditions.hypertension: Cured meats containing nitrites are usually high in sodium.

E251.name: Sodium Nitrate
E251.summary: Precursor to nitrite in cured meats; similar nitrosamine-related concerns at high consumption.

E260.name: Acetic Acid
E260.summary: Vinegar's active acid. No known safety concerns at food-use levels.

E270.name: Lactic Acid
E270.summary: Naturally occurring acid, widely used and well tolerated.

E290.name: Carbon Dioxide
E290.summary: Used for carbonation and packaging. No safety concerns.

E296.name: Malic Acid
E296.summary: Naturally occurring fruit acid, well tolerated.

E300.name: Ascorbic Acid (Vitamin C)
E300.summary: Vitamin C, also used as an antioxidant. No known safety concerns.

E322.name: Lecithin
E322.summary: Common emulsifier, usually soy- or sunflower-derived. Relevant for soy or egg allergy sufferers depending on source.

conditions.allergies: Source (soy, sunflower, or egg) should be checked if allergic to that ingredient.

E330.name: Citric Acid
E330.summary: Extremely common acidifier, generally recognised as safe.

E407.name: Carrageenan
E407.summary: Seaweed-derived thickener; some research suggests degraded carrageenan may irritate the gut, though food-grade carrageenan safety is still debated.

E415.name: Xanthan Gum
E415.summary: Common gluten-free thickener, generally well tolerated; very high intake can have a mild laxative effect.

E420.name: Sorbitol
E420.summary: Sugar alcohol; can cause bloating/diarrhoea in large amounts and affects blood sugar less than sugar, but still relevant for diabetics to track.

conditions.diabetes: Lower glycemic impact than sucrose but still contributes carbohydrate load; large amounts can cause GI upset.

E421.name: Mannitol
E421.summary: Sugar alcohol with a laxative effect at moderate doses; EU requires a 'excessive consumption may have a laxative effect' warning.

conditions.diabetes: Low glycemic impact, sometimes used as a sugar substitute.

E440.name: Pectin
E440.summary: Fruit-derived gelling agent used in jams. No known safety concerns.

E450.name: Diphosphates
E450.summary: Phosphate additive; high overall phosphate intake has been associated with cardiovascular and kidney concerns in some studies, especially for people with existing kidney disease.

conditions.hypertension: High phosphate additive intake has been studied in relation to cardiovascular risk.

E451.name: Triphosphates
E451.summary: Phosphate additive with similar considerations to E450.

conditions.hypertension: High phosphate additive intake has been studied in relation to cardiovascular risk.

E471.name: Mono- and Diglycerides of Fatty Acids
E471.summary: Extremely common emulsifier; can be plant or animal-derived, relevant for vegans/vegetarians and halal/kosher diets when source is unclear.

E500.name: Sodium Bicarbonate
E500.summary: Baking soda. No known safety concerns at food-use levels.

conditions.hypertension: Contains sodium; relevant if tracking total sodium intake.

E621.name: Monosodium Glutamate (MSG)
E621.summary: Long debated 'Chinese Restaurant Syndrome' has not been substantiated by controlled studies for the general population; a small subset of people report sensitivity (headache, flushing) at high single doses.

conditions.hypertension: Contains sodium, though typically less per gram than table salt for equivalent flavour intensity.

E622.name: Monopotassium Glutamate
E622.summary: Glutamate salt, used as a lower-sodium alternative to MSG.

E627.name: Disodium Guanylate
E627.summary: Often used with MSG to boost umami; derived from yeast extract or fish, relevant for vegetarian/vegan and some allergy diets.

conditions.allergies: Can be derived from fish; check source if allergic.

E631.name: Disodium Inosinate
E631.summary: Umami enhancer often derived from meat or fish; not suitable for strict vegetarians/vegans unless confirmed synthetic.

conditions.allergies: Can be derived from fish or meat; check source if allergic or vegetarian/vegan.

E950.name: Acesulfame K
E950.summary: Zero-calorie sweetener, ~200x sweeter than sugar. Considered safe by EFSA/FDA at approved intake levels; some ongoing research debate on very high, long-term consumption.

conditions.diabetes: Does not affect blood glucose; commonly used in 'diabetic-friendly' products.

E951.name: Aspartame
E951.summary: Widely used sweetener; IARC classified it as 'possibly carcinogenic' in 2023 based on limited evidence, while EFSA/FDA maintain current intake levels are safe. Must be avoided by people with phenylketonuria (PKU) as it contains phenylalanine.

conditions.diabetes: Does not affect blood glucose directly.
conditions.pku: Contains phenylalanine; unsafe for people with phenylketonuria and must carry a warning label.

E952.name: Cyclamate
E952.summary: Zero-calorie sweetener, banned in the US since 1970 over historical (later disputed) cancer concerns; still approved in the EU.

E954.name: Saccharin
E954.summary: Oldest artificial sweetener; early rodent bladder-cancer link was later found not applicable to humans and removed from the US carcinogen list.

E960.name: Steviol Glycosides (Stevia)
E960.summary: Plant-derived zero-calorie sweetener, generally considered safe.

conditions.diabetes: Does not affect blood glucose; popular natural alternative to artificial sweeteners.

E965.name: Maltitol
E965.summary: Sugar alcohol; noticeable laxative effect in moderate amounts, and has a higher glycemic impact than other sugar alcohols.

conditions.diabetes: Higher glycemic impact than most sugar alcohols; portion awareness advised.
